questions.question: Explain automatic type promotion in Java with an example.
keyPoints: Automatic type promotion ensures operands are converted to compatible types during operations
Example: int + double → double (int promoted to double)
This prevents data loss and ensures type safety
Also called widening conversion or upcasting

questions.question: What is Binary Numeric Promotion in Java? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: Binary Numeric Promotion occurs when two operands of different numeric types are used in binary operation
The smaller type is automatically promoted to the larger type
Example: int + double = double, byte + int = int
Example: char + int = int, float + double = double

questions.question: Why does Java promote byte and short to int in expressions?
keyPoints: CPU is optimized to process int operations most efficiently
byte and short have smaller ranges that can easily overflow
int provides sufficient range for most calculations
Ensures consistent and predictable results in arithmetic operations

questions.question: Explain Unary Numeric Promotion with examples.
keyPoints: Unary operators (+, -, ~, ++, --) cause automatic promotion of byte, short, and char to int
This happens even when the result is assigned back to the original type
Example: byte b = 10; b = b + 5; // compile error, needs cast
Example: char c = 'A'; c++; // actually works due to special case in JLS

questions.question: What will be the output? Explain: double d = 10/3; System.out.println(d);
keyPoints: Output will be 3.0
Division 10/3 happens first (both ints) giving integer result 3
Then 3 is assigned to double, becoming 3.0
To get 3.333..., use 10.0/3 or (double)10/3

questions.question: Differentiate between widening and narrowing conversions in Java.
keyPoints: Widening (automatic): byte→short→int→long→float→double - automatic by compiler
Narrowing (explicit): requires cast, e.g., double→int
Widening never loses data, narrowing may lose data
Example: int i = 10; long l = i; // widening; int j = (int)l; // narrowing

questions.question: What is the output of: System.out.println('A' + 1); and System.out.println('A' + '1'); Explain.
keyPoints: 'A' + 1 outputs 66 (char 'A' = 65 ASCII, promoted to int, 65+1=66)
Both chars are promoted to int in arithmetic expressions
Printed as integer values, not characters

questions.question: Write a Java program demonstrating type promotion in expressions. Include examples of byte, short, char, int, long, float, and double types. Explain each promotion that occurs.
keyPoints: Program shows byte/short promotion to int in arithmetic
Shows char promotion to int with ASCII values
Shows widening: int→long→float→double hierarchy
Shows binary promotion when mixed types used
Explains each result with promotion rules
Shows that assignment after operation requires casting for byte/short

questions.question: Explain all the rules of type promotion in Java expressions with suitable code examples for each case.
keyPoints: Rule 1: byte, short, char are promoted to int in any expression
Rule 2: Binary promotion: smaller type promoted to larger type
Rule 3: Order: byte→short→int→long→float→double
Rule 4: If either operand is double, other becomes double (and so on)
Rule 5: boolean is never promoted
Examples for each rule with code and explanation
Compilation error occurs because b1 + b2 promotes both to int
Result of expression is int, cannot be assigned to byte without cast
Fix: byte b3 = (byte)(b1 + b2);
Should explain range: byte range -128 to 127, b1+b2=30 fits
Also explain b++ works because of special Java rule (implicit cast)

questions.question: Discuss integer overflow and underflow in Java with examples. How does type promotion relate to this phenomenon?
keyPoints: Overflow: when value exceeds maximum range, wraps to minimum
Underflow: when value goes below minimum, wraps to maximum
Example: byte b = 127; b++; // becomes -128
Type promotion doesn't prevent overflow - it may actually cause it
int overflow wraps silently without warning
long doesn't overflow when adding to int (promoted to long first)
Important in financial calculations - use BigDecimal
